refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated response protocols to minimize mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP3) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time budgets per failure mode.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular design with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces, and (3) predictive condition monitoring with automated fault detection and response protocols, structured to meet the ≥0.97 availability target through defined recovery time budgets allocated to each identified failure mode.

potential_sources: Redundancy and modularity are orthogonal concerns: redundancy addresses fault masking; modularity addresses repair speed. Both are necessary and independently implementable.
Monitoring (DP3) is loosely coupled to redundancy and modularity—it informs maintenance decisions but does not require tight integration with failover logic.
Risk: If redundancy and modularity are not coordinated at the IM layer, redundant units may not be truly independent (e.g., shared power rails, coupled control logic), reducing effectiveness.
Risk: Predictive maintenance requires data flow from monitoring to maintenance scheduling; poor IM boundary definition could create tight coupling between sensing and maintenance planning.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is a composition of three semi-independent strategies. Coupling is manageable if each strategy is assigned clear ownership and interface contracts at the IM layer. The main risk is architectural drift where redundant units share hidden dependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined target availability (≥0.97) is achievable with the proposed architecture given worksite constraints (e.g., environmental conditions, maintenance access, spare parts availability).
Assumption: Failure modes and their frequencies are known or can be estimated from historical data or similar systems.
Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and space budgets.
Risk: If worksite safety policies restrict certain maintenance activities (e.g., hot-swap during operation), modularity benefits may be negated; MTTR could exceed recovery time budgets.
Risk: Predictive maintenance effectiveness depends on sensor reliability and data quality; poor sensor design or calibration could lead to false positives/negatives, undermining availability.
Risk: Distributed control and inter-node synchronization introduce complexity; synchronization failures or network latency could degrade availability rather than improve it.
Risk: Graceful degradation (DP4) is implicit in DP7 but not explicitly designed; unclear which functions are non-critical and how degraded operation is validated.
Risk: Recovery time budgets must be validated through testing; if actual MTTR exceeds budgets for any failure mode, target availability cannot be met.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP7 to explicitly decompose into three sub-DPs with clear ownership and interface contracts: (1) DP7a: Redundancy strategy (which subsystems, failover logic, synchronization protocol), (2) DP7b: Modularity and repair strategy (component boundaries, hot-swap interfaces, spare parts logistics), (3) DP7c: Monitoring and predictive maintenance strategy (sensor placement, degradation models, maintenance trigger thresholds). For each sub-DP, define a recovery time budget and validate that the sum of budgets across all failure modes supports the ≥0.97 availability target. Explicitly address graceful degradation: identify non-critical functions and define the operational envelope during degraded states. Establish a traceability matrix linking each failure mode to the sub-DP(s) that address it, ensuring no gaps. Validate assumptions about worksite constraints (safety policies, maintenance access, spare parts) early in design; if constraints are tighter than assumed, revise DP7 or escalate to customer for trade-off discussion.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability architecture encompassing redundancy management, modular component design with hot-swap capability, predictive condition monitoring, automated fault detection, and recovery orchestration across all subsystems to achieve ≥0.97 availability target

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound health and status data from Vehicle subsystems, Teleoperation systems, and Safety Services for condition monitoring and fault detection

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound recovery commands and failover directives to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services based on automated fault response protocols

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound critical safety notifications when availability thresholds are breached or unrecoverable faults are detected

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication for distributed redundancy coordination and inter-subsystem failover signaling

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level integrator responsible for cross-subsystem availability orchestration, redundancy coordination, and holistic fault management. Redundancy logic, failover automation, and condition monitoring are naturally system-level concerns. Minor diffusion: recovery time budget allocation requires coordination with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, but control logic remains centralized.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a subsystem, not a system-level integrator. DP7 requires cross-subsystem redundancy and failover orchestration that cannot be localized to Vehicle alone. Availability architecture must span Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. High diffusion: redundancy logic, predictive monitoring, and fault response protocols would scatter across multiple subsystems.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a subsystem focused on remote control and communication. DP7 is a system-level availability strategy that transcends any single subsystem. Containment would be incomplete and fragmented. High diffusion: redundancy, failover, and condition monitoring would require extensive coupling to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Weak containment: Safety Services handles safety-critical functions but is not the appropriate locus for system-wide availability orchestration. DP7 includes non-safety availability concerns (e.g., performance redundancy, modular hot-swap design) that fall outside Safety Services scope. High diffusion: availability logic would diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP7 is a system-level availability architecture that requires centralized orchestration of redundancy, failover, and condition monitoring across all subsystems. NGHE Solution, as the top-level system integrator, is the only module with sufficient scope and authority to contain the full DP without major diffusion. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are subsystems that will implement specific availability mechanisms (e.g., component redundancy, failover protocols) as directed by NGHE Solution's availability framework, but they do not own the integrated architecture itself.
containment_risks: Risk: Availability orchestration logic may become tightly coupled to subsystem-specific implementations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), reducing modularity and increasing maintenance burden.

Risk: Recovery time budget allocation and failure mode prioritization require deep knowledge of subsystem behavior; NGHE Solution must maintain accurate models of subsystem failure modes and recovery times.

Risk: Predictive condition monitoring may require real-time processing of high-volume telemetry; NGHE Solution must have sufficient computational capacity and low-latency access to Telemetry interface.
Risk: Automated fault response protocols must be validated against all failure mode combinations; incomplete coverage could leave unhandled failure scenarios.
Risk: Hot-swappable component design requires standardized interfaces across subsystems; interface standardization must be enforced at design time, not retrofitted.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly addresses the FR by specifying a coherent solution strategy: redundancy + modular design + predictive monitoring = availability ≥0.97. The DP is logically sound and maps clearly to the acceptance criterion (measured availability ≥0.97). The three pillars (redundancy, modularity, predictive monitoring) are necessary and jointly sufficient to achieve the target. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: CRITICAL: Recovery time budget allocation logic is distributed across NGHE Solution (orchestration) and subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Each subsystem must implement its allocated recovery time budget, but NGHE Solution must maintain authoritative models of subsystem recovery times. Misalignment between NGHE Solution's assumptions and actual subsystem behavior will cause availability target miss. Recommend explicit IM boundary for 'Recovery Time Budget Manager' with clear ownership of budget allocation, validation, and real-time tracking.

HIGH: Predictive condition monitoring and automated fault detection span multiple data sources (Vehicle telemetry, Teleoperation status, Safety Services alerts). NGHE Solution must aggregate, correlate, and interpret heterogeneous telemetry streams in real time. If monitoring logic is not centralized, fault detection latency will increase and false positives/negatives will proliferate. Recommend explicit IM boundary for 'Condition Monitoring Engine' with standardized telemetry schema and fault detection rules.

HIGH: Automated fault response protocols (failover directives, recovery commands) must be validated against all failure mode combinations. If response logic is scattered across NGHE Solution and subsystems, coverage gaps and race conditions will emerge. Recommend explicit IM boundary for 'Fault Response Orchestrator' with comprehensive failure mode matrix and validated response sequences.

MEDIUM: Hot-swappable component design requires standardized interfaces across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. If interface standardization is not enforced at design time, retrofitting will be costly and error-prone. Recommend explicit IM boundary for 'Interface Standardization Authority' with design-time validation gates.

MEDIUM: Redundancy logic (which subsystems are redundant, failover triggers, switchover timing) is centralized in NGHE Solution but depends on subsystem-specific knowledge (MTBF, recovery time, criticality). If subsystem models are not kept in sync with actual implementations, redundancy decisions will be suboptimal. Recommend explicit IM boundary for 'Redundancy Configuration Manager' with version control and change notification.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership candidate (containment score 0.92). However, the 0.08 diffusion score masks significant architectural risks. The DP is logically centralized but operationally distributed: NGHE Solution orchestrates, but Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services implement. This is acceptable if and only if: (1) subsystem responsibilities are explicitly defined in lower-level FRs/DPs, (2) interfaces between NGHE Solution and subsystems are formally specified (telemetry schema, command protocol, response timing), and (3) integration testing validates end-to-end availability across all failure modes. Current IM boundary definition is too coarse; recommend decomposing into sub-modules: Condition Monitoring Engine, Fault Response Orchestrator, Recovery Time Budget Manager, Redundancy Configuration Manager. Each sub-module should have explicit ownership, interface contracts, and acceptance criteria.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FEASIBLE with caveats. Acceptance criterion (measured availability ≥0.97) is quantitative and testable. However, verification requires: (1) agreed evaluation period (duration, operational profile, failure injection scenarios), (2) measurement methodology (uptime definition, downtime attribution, measurement accuracy), (3) failure mode coverage (which failure modes are in-scope for the 0.97 target, which are excluded). Current FR does not specify these details. Recommend adding acceptance criteria sub-clauses: 'Evaluation period: [X hours/days] of continuous operation under [defined operational profile]', 'Downtime attribution: [defined methodology for assigning downtime to specific failure modes]', 'Failure mode scope: [list of in-scope failure modes and their target recovery times]'.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: MODERATE. DP7 allocates recovery time budgets to each failure mode, but allocation methodology is not specified. Recommend adding DP sub-clause: 'Recovery time budget allocation shall be derived from failure mode analysis (FMEA) with criticality weighting: critical failures (safety-related) ≤ [X] seconds, major failures (mission-critical) ≤ [Y] seconds, minor failures (performance-degrading) ≤ [Z] seconds. Total downtime budget = sum of (failure probability × recovery time) across all failure modes, constrained to achieve ≥0.97 availability.' This will make allocation transparent and verifiable. Current IM boundary does not explicitly own this allocation; recommend assigning to 'Recovery Time Budget Manager' sub-module.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 (DP7 refinement): Add explicit sub-modules to DP7: '(1) Redundancy Architecture: [specify which subsystems are redundant, failover triggers, switchover timing], (2) Modular Design: [specify standardized interface requirements, hot-swap protocols, component lifecycle management], (3) Predictive Monitoring: [specify telemetry schema, condition indicators, fault detection rules, alert thresholds], (4) Automated Response: [specify fault response protocols, recovery sequences, timeout handling], (5) Recovery Time Budget: [specify allocation methodology, per-failure-mode budgets, real-time tracking].'

REVISION 2 (IM decomposition): Decompose NGHE Solution IM into sub-modules with explicit ownership: (a) Condition Monitoring Engine (owns telemetry aggregation, fault detection, alert generation), (b) Fault Response Orchestrator (owns failover logic, recovery command sequencing, timeout handling), (c) Recovery Time Budget Manager (owns budget allocation, real-time tracking, budget violation alerts), (d) Redundancy Configuration Manager (owns redundancy models, failover trigger definitions, switchover timing). Each sub-module should have explicit interfaces, acceptance criteria, and integration test cases.

REVISION 3 (FR acceptance criteria expansion): Add sub-clauses to FR: 'Evaluation period: [specify duration, e.g., 720 hours of continuous operation], Operational profile: [specify mission profile, environmental conditions, failure injection scenarios], Downtime attribution: [specify methodology for assigning downtime to specific failure modes, e.g., MTTR measurement, root cause analysis], Failure mode scope: [specify which failure modes are in-scope for the 0.97 target, which are excluded, e.g., 'excludes customer-induced shutdowns, includes all subsystem failures'].'

REVISION 4 (Interface specification): Formalize Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces with explicit contracts: (a) Telemetry: [specify data schema, sampling rate, latency SLA, loss tolerance], (b) Tasking: [specify command format, response time SLA, acknowledgment protocol], (c) SafetyAlert: [specify alert format, priority levels, delivery SLA], (d) Network Link: [specify bandwidth, latency, jitter, redundancy mechanism].

REVISION 5 (Validation strategy): Add acceptance criterion: 'Availability architecture shall be validated through: (1) FMEA with recovery time budget allocation, (2) failure mode injection testing covering [X]% of identified failure modes, (3) end-to-end integration testing across all subsystems, (4) stress testing under [defined load profile], (5) long-duration soak testing over [evaluation period] with continuous monitoring and root cause analysis of any downtime events.' This will make verification strategy explicit and testable.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass logically, but the DP-IM gate reveals critical architectural diffusion risks that must be resolved before decomposition. The gate analysis identifies five explicit IM sub-modules (Condition Monitoring Engine, Fault Response Orchestrator, Recovery Time Budget Manager, Redundancy Configuration Manager, Interface Standardization Authority) that are currently implicit in the coarse NGHE Solution boundary. The containment score of 0.92 masks a 0.08 diffusion risk that is operationally significant: NGHE Solution orchestrates centrally but depends on distributed subsystem implementations. This creates a critical failure mode: misalignment between NGHE Solution's assumptions (subsystem recovery times, telemetry schemas, failover triggers) and actual subsystem behavior will cause availability target miss. Safety criticality is high, making this architectural risk unacceptable to carry forward without explicit resolution. Revising DP7 and IM boundaries now prevents costly rework during detailed design and integration. The FR acceptance criteria also require expansion to make verification feasible (evaluation period, operational profile, downtime attribution methodology, failure mode scope). Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition without these revisions would propagate ambiguity into lower levels and create verification gaps.

revise.why: DP7 must be refined to explicitly specify the five architectural sub-modules identified in the DP-IM gate analysis. Each sub-module must have: (1) clear ownership (assigned to NGHE Solution or subsystem), (2) explicit interface contracts (telemetry schema, command protocol, response timing), (3) acceptance criteria (e.g., fault detection latency ≤ X ms, recovery time budget allocation methodology, redundancy configuration validation). IM boundary for NGHE Solution must be decomposed from a single coarse module into five explicit sub-modules: (a) Condition Monitoring Engine (telemetry aggregation, fault detection, alert generation), (b) Fault Response Orchestrator (failover logic, recovery sequencing, timeout handling), (c) Recovery Time Budget Manager (budget allocation, real-time tracking, budget violation alerts), (d) Redundancy Configuration Manager (redundancy models, failover triggers, switchover timing), (e) Interface Standardization Authority (design-time validation of subsystem interfaces). Additionally, FR acceptance criteria must be expanded with sub-clauses specifying: evaluation period (e.g., 720 hours), operational profile (mission profile, environmental conditions, failure injection scenarios), downtime attribution methodology (MTTR measurement, root cause analysis), and failure mode scope (in-scope vs. excluded failure modes). These revisions will eliminate the 0.08 diffusion risk and make the architecture verifiable.

assumptions: NGHE Solution can be decomposed into five explicit sub-modules without violating system-level constraints (latency, bandwidth, computational load).
Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can implement standardized telemetry schemas and command protocols without major redesign.
Recovery time budgets can be allocated per failure mode using FMEA with criticality weighting, and subsystems can commit to meeting allocated budgets.
Failure mode injection testing can be performed safely in a controlled environment without risking system safety.
Evaluation period of 720+ hours is feasible within project schedule and budget.
Downtime attribution methodology can be automated via instrumentation and root cause analysis tools.
open_risks: CRITICAL: If subsystem recovery times drift from NGHE Solution's assumptions during operation, availability target will miss. Mitigation: implement Recovery Time Budget Manager with real-time tracking and budget violation alerts; establish change notification protocol between subsystems and NGHE Solution.

CRITICAL: If telemetry schemas are not standardized across subsystems, Condition Monitoring Engine will experience high false positive/negative rates and increased fault detection latency. Mitigation: enforce Interface Standardization Authority with design-time validation gates; establish telemetry schema versioning and backward compatibility requirements.

HIGH: If Fault Response Orchestrator logic is not validated against all failure mode combinations, race conditions and coverage gaps will emerge. Mitigation: perform FMEA with explicit failure mode matrix; conduct failure mode injection testing covering ≥90% of identified failure modes; perform end-to-end integration testing across all subsystems.

HIGH: If hot-swappable component design is not enforced at design time, retrofitting will be costly and error-prone. Mitigation: assign Interface Standardization Authority explicit ownership; establish design-time validation gates; conduct design reviews with subsystem teams.

MEDIUM: If evaluation period is too short or operational profile does not reflect real-world conditions, measured availability may not be representative. Mitigation: define evaluation period and operational profile in collaboration with stakeholders; conduct stress testing and soak testing under realistic conditions; perform root cause analysis on any downtime events.

MEDIUM: If downtime attribution methodology is not clearly defined, disputes may arise about whether downtime is attributable to system design or external factors (e.g., customer-induced shutdowns). Mitigation: establish explicit downtime attribution rules in FR acceptance criteria; automate downtime attribution via instrumentation; conduct independent verification of downtime attribution methodology.
evidence_needed: Detailed FMEA with failure mode matrix, criticality classification, recovery time budget allocation, and validation of budget allocation methodology.
Formal interface specifications for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link with explicit data schemas, sampling rates, latency SLAs, and loss tolerances.

Design specifications for five IM sub-modules (Condition Monitoring Engine, Fault Response Orchestrator, Recovery Time Budget Manager, Redundancy Configuration Manager, Interface Standardization Authority) with ownership, acceptance criteria, and integration test cases.
Failure mode injection test plan covering ≥90% of identified failure modes with test scenarios, expected outcomes, and pass/fail criteria.
End-to-end integration test plan validating availability across all subsystems under defined operational profile.
Stress testing and soak testing plan with load profiles, duration, and success criteria.
Downtime attribution methodology specification with automated instrumentation and root cause analysis procedures.
Evaluation period specification (duration, operational profile, environmental conditions, failure injection scenarios) with justification for feasibility within project schedule and budget.
Subsystem commitment letters confirming recovery time budget allocations and interface standardization compliance.
Design review records from subsystem teams confirming hot-swappable component design and interface standardization.
